summary: This 2017 post clarifies the distinctions between data warehouses and data lakes, arguing against the prevailing notion that data lakes will replace data warehouses. Späti explains that data warehouses serve structured, slowly-changing analytical needs with ETL processes, while data lakes handle unstructured, fast-changing big data with ELT approaches. He advocates for maintaining both environments in modern organizations, using data lakes for exploration and data warehouses for structured, accessible reporting. The post also introduces Data Vault methodology as a bridge between the two paradigms and promotes Data Warehouse Automation tools as the future of DWH development.
key_points: Data warehouses and data lakes serve fundamentally different purposes: DWH for cleansed, structured analytics; data lakes for raw, unstructured exploration
ETL belongs to data warehouses (transform then load), while ELT belongs to data lakes (load then transform)
Big data extends and enriches data warehouses but cannot replace them—it's the traditional way of building DWH that's dead, not the concept itself
Data Vault methodology offers a flexible approach that combines benefits of both paradigms for adapting to changing business environments
Organizations should import valuable patterns discovered in data lakes back into data warehouses to make insights accessible to operational users
money_quotes: If you think of a DWH as a store of bottled water – cleansed and packaged and structured for easy consumption – the data lake is a large body of water in a more natural state.
Big data can extend and enrich a Data Warehouse, but cannot replace it.
It is not Data Warehouses that are dead, but the traditional way of designing and building them.
More data is always better but can also lead to more chaotic data stores where nobody knows what's in there.
The Data Warehouse is designed for slowly changing data; Data Lakes are designed for quickly changing data.
Your ETL tool was probably outdated before you were ready to deploy it.
key_insight: Data warehouses and data lakes complement rather than compete—organizations need both, with data lakes for exploration and data warehouses for making insights accessible and structured for everyday business users.
